coredata.dc:title: Production of pristine graphene quantum dots from graphite by a shear-mixer in supercritical CO2
coredata.dc:creator: Alidad, Farnaz
Navik, Rahul
Gai, Yanzhe
Zhao, Yaping
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots exhibit unique electronic and optical properties due to their combined properties of quantum confinements and edge effects and have broad applications like bio-imaging, drug delivery, biosensors and photovoltaic applications. In this paper, we presented a facile and green preparation of pristine graphene quantum dots (PGQDs) from graphite using a shear-assisted supercritical CO2 approach. The effects of the pressure, graphite amount, and shearing speed on the yield of the PGQDs were investigated. The fluorescence quantum yield of the PGQDs achieved 8.6%, and it was highly stable with blue light emission. The mechanism for forming the PGQDs was elucidated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Supercritical CO2 exfoliation
Shear mixer
Graphite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experiment process of the preparation of the PGQDs
Effects of experimental parameters on PGQDs yield
Effect of the speed of shear mixer
Analysis and characterizations of the PGQDs
Mechanism of the formation of PGQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental equipment drawing: 1 – CO2 cylinder, 2 – chilling unit, 3– high-pressure CO2 pump, 4 – stainless-steel reactor, 5 – shear mixer, 6 – sampling bottle. TI: a Temperature indicator; PI: Pressure indicator, V1—V3 – needle valves.
Influence of process parameters on the PGQDs yield: (a) Supercritical CO2 pressure, (b) starting graphite amount, (c) shearing speed, (d) temperature.

Characterization of the PGQD: (a) TEM image of PGQDs obtained by treatment for 3 h. (b) The size distribution. (c) An HRTEM image is displaying graphene fringes. Inset SAED pattern. (d) AFM image. (e) The height profile along the lines shown in panel d. (f) The histogram of the thickness distribution. (g) XRD patterns of graphite and PGQDs. (h) Raman spectra of graphite and PGQDs.

Optical characterization of PGQDs: (a) UV–visible absorption spectrum of the PGQDs dispersed in ethanol. (b) PL spectra of the PGQDs at different excitation wavelengths (c) PL-excitation (@λem = 431 nm) and PL-emission (@λex = 372 nm) spectra of PGQDs. (d) Stability of PGQDs over time, an optical photograph of the sample (Right) and blank sample (left), (1) under daylight, (2) and (3) illuminated by UV lamp at 365 nm, after 0 month and 9 months of production, respectively.
Schematic drawing of the exfoliation and cutting mechanism of graphite into PGQDs by a shear-mixer in supercritical CO2.

head.ce:title: Production of pristine graphene quantum dots from graphite by a shear-mixer in supercritical CO2

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, 800 Dong Chuan Road, Shanghai 200240, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots exhibit unique electronic and optical properties due to their combined properties of quantum confinements and edge effects and have broad applications like bio-imaging, drug delivery, biosensors and photovoltaic applications. In this paper, we presented a facile and green preparation of pristine graphene quantum dots (PGQDs) from graphite using a shear-assisted supercritical CO2 approach. The effects of the pressure, graphite amount, and shearing speed on the yield of the PGQDs were investigated. The fluorescence quantum yield of the PGQDs achieved 8.6%, and it was highly stable with blue light emission. The mechanism for forming the PGQDs was elucidated.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), zero-dimensional luminescent graphene nanoparticles, have attracted more and more attention since the discovery of graphene because they possess unique electronic and optical properties owing to their edge effects and quantum confinement when their sizes are down to 10 nm [1]. The GQDs have superior advantages over the traditional semiconductor-based quantum dots (QDs) in terms of the chemical inertness, low cytotoxicity, large surface area, high transparency, tunable bandgap, low photobleaching, excellent solubility, eco-friendly nature, ease of functionalization and excellent biocompatibility [1,2]. Thus, the GQDs have significantly potential applications in many fields, such as catalysis and photovoltaic devices [2,3], bio-imaging [4], drug delivery [5,6], biosensors [7], capacitors [8], batteries [9,10], solar cells [11,12], light-emitting diodes, energy conversion, and bioanalysis [13]. The preparation of the GQDs is a prerequisite for their practical applications. The bottom-up process is one of the approaches, in which the GQDs were synthesized from the molecular precursors by chemical reactions, such as 4-bromobenzene carboxylic acid [14], hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene [15], 3-iodo-4-bromoaniline [16], glucose [17], citric acid [18]. The top-down process is another primary method, in which cheap bulk carbonaceous materials are often used as starting materials. Such as the graphite was first to be oxidized to graphite oxide sheets by a modified Hummers method [13] and then converted to the GQDs using either hydrothermal cutting [19], solvothermal cutting [20], microwave-assisted cutting [21] or electrochemical cutting [22]. However, the GQDs produced by the methods aforementioned contain oxygen group, and the involved reaction conditions are complex, harsh and time-consuming. Very few papers on the production of the PGQDs have been published [23].

In this work, we presented a shear-assisted supercritical CO2 approach to preparing the PGQDs with stable blue excitation-independent photoluminescence behavior directly from graphite powder for the first time. The influences of the process parameters on the PGQD yield were investigated. The thickness and size of the as-prepared PGQD were characterized by AFM and TEM. The fluorescence quantum yield of the PGQD was evaluated by Time-Resolved Fluorescence Spectrofluorometer.

Graphite powder (99.99%) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Carbon dioxide (99.9%) was obtained from Shanghai High-tech Co., Ltd. (China). Absolute ethanol (99.7%) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used without any further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiment process of the preparation of the PGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A schematic drawing of the equipment system for the production of the PGQDs from graphite using a shear mixer in supercritical CO2 is displayed in Fig. 1 . The system consists of a home-made high-pressure stainless steel reactor and a shear mixer, CO2 feeding system, a chiller, a temperature-controller, a pressure indicator and a product collector. The shear mixer consists of a 6-blade rotor and a 12-blade stator.

During experiments, a certain amount of natural graphite powder was placed in the reactor. Then the reactor was filled with CO2 via the manual pump and heated until it achieved to the desired conditions. After that, the shear mixer was started for 3 h with constant stirring. The pressure and temperature of the reactor were displayed by indicators. When the preset processing time ended, the product was gathered by opening a vent valve and directly sprayed with CO2 into a 200 ml conical flask containing absolute ethanol solvent. Resultant suspensions were subjected to centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 60 min to remove the graphite fragments. The supernatant was collected by a pipette for characterizations, which was the targeted PGQDs sample.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F) and Atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker ICON) was used to detect the size, morphology and layer number of the resultant samples. A few milliliters of the PGQDs dispersion was deposited dropwise onto the ultrathin carbon-coated copper grid and then dried for TEM characterization. Samples for AFM imaging were prepared via dropping the PGQDs dispersion onto freshly cleaved mica substrate and dried at 110 °C to evaporate ethanol then kept in a desiccator. The content of the PGQDs ethanol aqueous dispersion was measured using an UV–Visible spectrophotometer at 375 nm wavelength in the wavelength range of 200–400 nm (PC 756, Shanghai Spectrum Instruments Co., Ltd., China). Photoluminescence (PL) measurements and fluorescence quantum yield of the PGQDs were performed on steady state and Time-Resolved Fluorescence Spectrofluorometer (Photon technology international, Inc. PerkinElmer). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was examined on a BRUKER D8 Advance X-ray Diffraction apparatus (Cu Kα copper radiation of λ = 0.15405 nm) The analysis conditions were irradiated at 10–70° with a scan rate of 5° min−1. Raman spectra was carried out on a Dispersive Raman Microscope (Sentara R200-L, Bruker Optics) with a 532 nm laser source. Samples for Raman Spectroscopy were prepared by dropping the dispersion on a silicon substrate.

The ability of molecules to emit the absorbed light energy is characterized by the luminescence quantum yield (QY), which is defined as the fraction of total energy emitted per quantum of energy absorbed. The QY allows us to evaluate the sensitivity of the fluorimetric determination of the materials. The quantum yield of the samples was calculated by comparison of the integrated photoluminescence intensities and the absorbency values of the PGQDs with the references quinine sulfate.

Quinine sulfate (φ = 0.58) was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (refractive index (η) of 1.33). The PGQDs sample was dissolved in ethanol (η = 1.361). A quartz cuvette with a path length of 1.00 cm was used during the UV–Vis and PL measurement. The absorbency of the samples was controlled under 0.05 at the wavelengths of 370 nm and 350 nm, respectively. The quantum yield was calculated using Equation (1) below.
(1)
Q =
Q R
I
I R
A R
A
n
2
n
R
2
where Q is the QY, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, and n is the refractive index (1.36 for ethanol and 1.33 for water). The subscript R refers to the reference dye of the known QY.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of experimental parameters on PGQDs yield
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, we combined the high permeability nature of supercritical CO2 and the shearing force of the shear-mixer to produce the PGQDs directly from natural graphite. We explored the influence of the pressure, graphite amount, the speed of the shear mixer, and temperature on the PGQDs yield while fixed the processing time of 3 h. The yield of the PGQDs was calculated based on the mass ratio of the obtained PGQDs to the starting graphite amount. The results are shown in Fig. 2 .

As shown in Fig. 2a, the pressure affected the yield significantly. The yield increased from 0.54% up to 1.84% with increasing pressure from 8 MPa to 18 MPa when the temperature, the graphite amount, and the speed of the shear mixer were fixed at 45 °C, 0.5 g, and 3000 rpm, respectively. It can be attributed to that more CO2 molecules penetrated into the interlayer of graphite generating more repulsive free energy barrier with increasing pressure [24]. Then, the interlayer distance among the graphite sheets were expanded, and at the same time, the shear force generated by the large velocity gradient of the fluid cut the sheets into small particles and resulting in the formation of the PGQDs.

Initial amounts of the graphite ranging from 0.5 g to 3 g were used to determine the influence of the starting graphite amount on the yield. As shown in Fig. 2b, it can be observed that the yield decreased from 1.8% to 0.52% when the graphite amount increased. This result indicates that the molecular ratio of CO2 to graphite considerably affects the process. When the molar ratio was small, there are not enough CO2 molecules to intercalate the graphite interlayers to exfoliate the graphite [25] and to stabilize the formed PGQDs. According to the volume of the reactor vessel (500 mL) and the corresponding density of CO2 (0.7833 g/ml at the pressure of 18 MPa and the temperature of 45 °C), we calculate the mass of CO2 to be 392 g. When the pristine graphite amount used in the experiment was 0.5 g, the molar ratio of CO2 to graphite was 214, and the yield reached the maximum value of 1.8%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the speed of shear mixer
ce:section.ce:para: The shearing speed is a significant factor that affects the yield because it directly determines the magnitude of shear stress, resulting in the extent of graphite exfoliation and cutting of graphene sheets into PGQDs. As shown in Fig. 2c, the yield increased up to 1.8% at a shearing speed of 3000 rpm. While the pressure, the graphite amount and the shearing time were fixed at 18 MPa, 0.5 g, 3 h, respectively. It can be ascribed to that the shear force increased with increasing shearing speed to generate the PGQDs.

As shown in Fig. 2d, the PGQDs yield increases from 1% to 1.8% with an increase of temperature from 33 to 45 °C at the fixed other parameters, such as a graphite mass of 0.5 g, a pressure of 18 MPa and a shearing speed of 3000 rpm. However, the absolute increasing value reduced from 39 to 45 °C. Therefore, we did not test at the temperature higher than 45 °C. The reason for increasing yield with temperature might be that more energy was put in the graphite and CO2 molecules, which favored exfoliation of the graphite resulting in higher yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis and characterizations of the PGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM was carried out to characterize the size and morphology of the PGQDs. The TEM image of the as-prepared PGQDs shown in Fig. 3 a displays that they were spherical and homogeneous dispersion. Fig. 3b illustrates that the size distribution was in the range of 2.5–6 nm, most of which were around 4 nm. The HRTEM image of the individual PGQDs in Fig. 3c displayed high crystallinity of the PGQDs and the distinct lattice spacing of 0.24 nm which represents the [1 1 2 0] lattice fringes of graphene [26]. Also, the selected area diffraction pattern of the insert of Fig. 3c displays the crystalline nature of the PGQDs.

AFM is a nondestructive device for identification of the layer number by measuring the step height profile of the sample. Fig. 3d displays a typical AFM image of the obtained PGQDS on a mica substrate, and Fig. 3e shows the height profile along the line drawn in 3d. As shown in Fig. 3f, the histogram of the thickness distribution of the PGQDs derived from the step height of measuring100 particles of PGQD in the AFM images, which indicates that the particle thickness is in the range of 0.5–3 nm corresponding monolayer to less than 7 layers [27].

The crystallinity of the PGQDs can be explored with the help of X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns. As shown in Fig. 3g, the graphite exhibits a prominent (0 0 2) signal centered around 26° corresponding to the typical 0.334 nm interlayer spacing, while the PGQDs display a weak board (0 0 2) pick around 25°. It demonstrates the disordered stacking of the PGQDs with sp2 carbon structures [9,10].

Raman spectroscopy is an often used tool to identify the layer number and defect of graphene. Fig. 3h displays the Raman spectra of the produced PGQDs and the contrast graphite deposited, respectively, on a Si/SiO2 wafer recorded at a 532 nm wavelength. Two characteristic peaks, the G peak (1582 cm−1) and the 2D peak (2680 cm−1), can be seen. The G peak can be ascribed to the doubly degenerate zone center E2g mode, which is associated with phonon vibrations in sp2 carbon atoms. The 2D peak is derived from the inelastic scattering of second-order zone boundary phonons. From the shape and peak position of the 2D band, the PGQD sheets are considered to consist of monolayer and few layers [29]. The D peak (1337 cm−1) might be attributed the raw graphite and the edge defects of the PGQD due to its small size. Even though, the relative intensity ratio ID/IG (0.41) is much small, indicating the PGQD has low defects and high crystallinity degree [30]. These results are consistent with that of AMF and TEM.

The optical properties of the as-prepared PGQDs were studied in ethanol solution. The GQDs generally have absorption bands in the UV region with an absorption peak around 230 nm due to the π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [19,21]. The UV absorption pattern of the as-synthesized PGQDs is exhibited in Fig. 4 a. It can be seen that there is a distinct absorption peak at 225 nm, which is due to the blue-shifted π–π* transition of the aromatic CC bonds. Moreover, we found a weak peak at 275 nm wavelength. It can be attributed to n–π* transition in one or several functional groups such as CO, suggesting the PGQDs might have a sparse amount of oxygen-group.

The most distinctive feature of the PGQDs is their specific photoluminescence (PL) emission. To further investigate the optical properties of the PGQDs, the PL was measured at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 340 to 390 nm. Fig. 4b shows that the PL spectrum exerted the strongest peaks ∼407 nm, 431 nm, and a shoulder peak at 455 nm upon excitation at the wavelength of 370 nm. It is noticeable that the intensity of the PL reached the maximum value and then decreased, but the PL emission position remained invariable. This phenomenon indicates that the as-prepared PGQDs show an excitation-independent PL feature which is different from the other common GQDs which mostly had excitation-wavelength-dependent PL behavior which was attributed to the presence of defects [31]. It suggests that the synthesized particles in this work do not have any significant defect states and its PL origin is different from other GQDs. Fig. 4c shows the photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and PL spectra of the as-prepared PGQDs which exhibit intense blue emission exhibiting two PL peaks at 407 nm, 431 nm and a shoulder peak at 455 nm and PLE peak at 372 nm.

The as-obtained PGQDs in ethanol exhibited good aqueous dispersant and appeared colorless under natural light, also displayed excellent PL stability for 9 months, without any sedimentation or agglomeration. It can be seen from Fig. 4c that the PGQDs sample showed almost the same blue PL under the excitation of 365 nm UV light for over the period of 9 months. This PL stability demonstrated that the free zigzag edge sites were not responsible for the observed PL [27]. The blue luminescence of the PGQDs is dominated by intrinsic states in the highly crystalline structure.

As shown in Table 1 , the fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of the PGQDs was measured to be 8.6% by comparing the integrated PL intensity of PGQDs to that of a standard quinine sulfate which has the quantum yield of 58% [32]. It is higher than that made by chemical reduction of GO and the hydrothermal deoxidization treatment, which is only 7.5% [33]. Our method of producing PGQDs make not only high QY but also has distinct advantages over other methods in terms of without using acid, base and oxidant. Also, the procedure is simple and can be scaleable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the formation of PGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A schematic drawing of the formation of the PGQDs by a shear-mixer in the supercritical CO2 fluid is shown in Fig. 5 . When the reactor placed with the graphite (Fig. 5a) was filled with CO2, and the CO2 reached its supercritical state, the CO2 molecules penetrated into the interlayers of the graphite due to its high diffusivity, low viscosity, zero interfacial tension and small molecule size (Fig. 5b). The CO2 molecules accumulated in the graphitic interlayer resulted in decreasing van der Waals forces between adjacent graphite layers. When the rotor ran at high speed, the supercritical CO2 fluid was forced into the rotor region resulting in high-speed fluid flowing in the gap between the rotor and the stator (Fig. 5c). Then, the cavitation and collision during shearing exfoliated and cut the graphite. Thus, under the coupling actions of the supercritical CO2, the generated cavitation and collision occurred in the reactor. Hence the graphite was exfoliated and cut down into the PGQDs (Fig. 5d). After spraying the exfoliated and cut the sample into the ethanol and centrifuging, the resultant samples were the PGQDs (Fig. 5e). The fluid dynamic force coupled with the effect of supercritical CO2 is the primary reason for the exfoliation and fragmentation of the graphite powder into the PGQDs.

We successfully developed a facile and green approach to produce the PGQDs for the first time. Taking natural graphite as starting materials, the PGQDs can be prepared directly via only one step using a shear-mixer in supercritical CO2 without using any molecular capping agent in few hours. During the exfoliation process, system pressure, starting graphite amount, shearing speed and process temperature were all proven to have the impressive impact on the PGQDs yields. The obtained PGQDs are around 4 nm in lateral size with less than 7 graphene layers. The PGQDs displayed a striking excitation-independent PL behavior which suggests that the synthesized particles do not have any significant defect states. The blue luminescence of the as-synthesized PGQDs is stable for 9 months, and the PL quantum yield achieved 8.6% measured against the quinine sulfate standard. This fast-processing, low-cost, and environmentally friendly approach has potential application in the production of high-quality pristine quantum dots.
We are thankful for the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21576165), and Ms. Huiqin Li of Instrumental Analysis Center of SJTU for analysis.
The authors declare no conflict of interest.
